

[Shawn] Hey, it’s Shawn and André today. This is our final review for the Art of Email workshop. Feeling a little nostalgic, a little bittersweet today. Apparently… Just so everyone understands how we approach these things, André looked through this and spent some time with it, made some comments. I haven’t seen it yet because I like to have a sort of fresh reaction. We just did a quick overview of what we’re looking at. I’m laughing because this is, if I’m not mistaken, this is another email series that the target audience are women. Women on dating sites. So two dudes talking about emails for women on dating sites should be wildly entertaining again today. Last time André wore a dress. I don’t know if he did this time as well. No judgment, of course. But let me see if I understand what you just told me, André, and then I’m gonna tee this up for you to give your sort of high-level thoughts, and then we’re gonna see where it leads. I’m curious to read some of this ’cause now I’m intrigued. All right, so my understanding is that there are two bridge emails. And those bridge emails are designed to create an interest list, which then gets five… There are five different emails on Monday through Friday. And that sequence is getting people to sign up for a masterclass. It’s a webinar masterclass, where conversion for that is moving on to a call, I think. And then, in addition, there’s a show-up sequence. Only people who register for the masterclass-slash-webinar would get this show-up sequence. And then there’s a sequence to be written for the post-masterclass. And I think what we’re going to focus on today are just the bridge emails. But did I get that high-level description right of how this is set up? I’m sort of looking at it here. Does that match up with your understanding of it?

[André] Yeah, so that’s how it was explained. So the sequence is written by the content manager for the face of the business, who is Anna, or Anna. I’m not sure how to pronounce that. And Khara is the one who… The content manager who wrote this. So she’s writing for Anna through her lens. So this is all first-person, right? From Anna’s point of view, even though she hasn’t written these, Anna. And so you can see at the top, this is how… So this actually went out already. So there’s the bridge to get onto the interest list, and then there’s these emails, as Shawn said, to get into the webinar. And so I’ve read the bridges and then a few of the registration-sequence emails. I’ve not read anything else because that’s kind of irrelevant right now. I think the power of this thing is the strategy because the tactics really are a downstream of strategy. And there’s many different strategies on how to execute on a promotion, for example. And I think there’s a different way to do this particular one. And whenever Shawn and I are doing promotions, we always spend a lot of time… Maybe, yeah… I mean, I was gonna say, maybe as much time as actually writing the emails. But we spend a lot of time backwards and forwards, trying to figure out, what’s the best way to get this thing out there? What’s the best way to package it up? To frame it, to… What’s the theme? All these things, before we’ve even written a single word, go first. So I needed to get into the mind of an audience that I’m, you know, I’m clearly not the audience. I can wear dresses and miniskirts all day long. I’m still not gonna be this audience. So…

[Shawn] Hold on a minute. I’m trying to recover. I’m trying to put Clorox eye drops in my eyes now to bleach out this idea of you wearing a miniskirt. It’s just horrifying. So, I apologize.

– Shawn’s just jealous.

[Shawn] Let me just apologize to everyone in our audience on behalf of André for putting that image in your minds. My word. Yeah, so, I mean… So there’s a business-model question, or not a business model. There’s a there’s an offer-structure question here, too. And this is a common, common structure of a of a promotion, right? So you see the infinite number of ways to do… Of having a sequence that sends people to a webinar. Calling a webinar a masterclass is always a good idea. Don’t call webinars “webinars”. And then to a phone close. So do we know the price point for this? I’m guessing if it’s a phone close, it’s a high price point. Do we know that? Maybe we don’t know that. I don’t know.

[André] Um, yeah. I think the product itself is a four-and-a-half-thousand dollar group coaching with lifetime access. That’s my understanding. So they don’t… I don’t think they sell that directly. So people need to get onto a call with Anna, and then that happens, but that’s outside the scope of this.

[Shawn] Okay, so, yeah. But it does have an impact, right? Because when you think about all the decisions being made here that will change the dynamics of everything that follows… So decision number one, bridge email or no bridge email. Big. Very significant downstream consequences. Because no bridge email, you’re talking to everyone, a bridge email, you’re talking to people who are already interested. I don’t know if the masterclass is live only. That certainly has an impact too. Like, if everybody has to show up. I’m guessing because they have a show-up sequence, they do. So they register for the masterclass. And then there’s only a point in time. I don’t know if there’s also the ability for somebody to watch a replay. So that’s, that’s important as well. I mean, there are lots of decisions that have consequences. And I’m guessing we’re not being asked for that level of insight. So where do you wanna tackle this? Do you wanna just jump on the bridge emails? Like, you read through them. What do you think is like… what’s the inflection point that’s most valuable for our audience for us to just start jamming on?

[André] So the first thing I was looking at is obviously just to wrap my head around decisions that have been made. Why they’ve structured it this way. And the fact that I think structuring it in a different way would be a better decision. And then also, I think it’s worth mentioning that this was the first time they’ve ever done the bridge email. So, historically, the only way they’ve ever done any promotion or anything, it goes out to everybody. So everybody gets everything whenever anything goes out. So this, they were apprehensive about. Or excited-slash-apprehensive. You know, this is the first time they’ve done the bridge. So we can… I’ve got their permission that we can just mention what those numbers were, which we can do just now. So the bridge is the first thing to look at. And obviously when Shawn and I are doing this ourselves, one of the most important things for us is, you know, what’s the lead, the hook, the thematic arc that we’re going to run across this thing. Obviously, I didn’t know any of this. Well, neither of us know this. I had to go reading to try and find that. And I found something later on in the second bridge email that feels like it’s the thing that should be right up here at the top, but it’s not. So I didn’t read that… I didn’t see that on my first read, which is… You can kind of see my comment on the side here. It says, “Second-read comments”, because I didn’t know. I just started to read, and to try and figure out what that was. So that was the first thing.

[Shawn] And it’s hard, too. I’m looking at this. You’ve highlighted something, and I know…

[André] You don’t want to read it, do you?

[Shawn] No, I know. I know I’ve written that exact same sentence before, right? “Here’s a little context before I share the time-sensitive details.” Like, “Oh, shit.” It’s so hard. I was working on something yesterday and it’s, it’s so hard to craft a good hook. I mean, it’s just… That reminds me of Hemingway’s comment that writing is easy. You just sit down at a typewriter and bleed. Writing a good hook is just… Ugh, it can be, it can be bleeding. I think, here, the hooks are already there. ‘Cause, just for everyone listening, the bridge starts with, “Hey, it’s Anna. I have some exciting news and an opportunity for you. Here’s a little context before I share the time-sensitive details.” And then it’s, oh, one, two, three, three different quotes. So, you know, clearly from other people. “I seem to attract all the wrong men, all the nice guys who are sweethearts, but I tend to walk all over them. So unattractive. I’m losing hope. I’m being ghosted all the time, Where are all the good men?” Pick one of these and lead with it. That’s the hook, right? I mean, to me, that’s the thing that… Assuming that one of these… My guess is that the three quotes that were chosen here are representative of three, like, the three pillars of what the audience feels. That would be a way to write a bridge email so that you’re, you’re hitting that in the beginning. But this is made less powerful by the setup. That’s not always true, but sometimes the setup amplifies. And maybe, you know, maybe it does because, “Hey, I have some exciting news and an opportunity.” I could talk myself into a couple of different things. But I think one of these… The challenge I’m having with this is that none of these jump out at me as grabbing me by the throat. That I think, like, “Oh, that.” But if one of these was the thing, like, if losing hope and being ghosted was the thing that most people are struggling with in her audience, that’s what I would lead with. I would just say that. Before I even say, “Hey, it’s Anna.” I would just have this quote and then say… Then the lead-in is, it’s the quote. And then it’s, you know, Anna starts talking about how she hears this all the time, right? And then, then it transitions from there. Maybe. That’s one way to think about doing this. But this just doesn’t… I’m shining the light right on myself for this too, because I know how many times I’ve done this. This could be a much more powerful lead, a much more powerful hook. It just becomes a matter of, do you… You, Khara. Anna. Do you have, do you have the hook? Has somebody said the thing? Can you put it in one line? That your audience who really feels this is going to look at it and go, “Oh. Yeah. That’s it to me too.” You know, I’m going to make something up again. Not only am I not the target audience, I’ve been married 25 years. So I have never used a dating app in my life. I went out… I’m not… I intellectually can understand what I’m reading, but I’m not emotionally able to process it with any degree of efficacy. So just keep that in mind. But I think there’s something here. The holidays are coming up, right? I’m guessing that the holidays are probably a time when, you know, relationship stuff… I don’t know if people are more sensitive to that. But pick something where it’s clear the thing that you’re saying right now is what they’re feeling right now. And I don’t know if that has anything to do with the holidays, but I could imagine a lead that’s something like… Some sort of statement that’s around this idea of, you know, you’re staring down the barrel of the holiday season again, and it’s… You’re staring down the barrel of the holiday season and, once again, you’re going to be going to these, you know, whatever… Going to the holiday party alone, or bringing some, using her language, “a nice guy or an asshole” to the Christmas party. I dunno if that’s it all, but there’s something timely and visceral, and how are they feeling right now? That should be the lead, I think. Right. Up to you, dress guy.

[André] Miniskirt, by the way.

[Shawn] Why? Why would you do that?

[André] What’s wrong? The sun’s shining, at least.

– That’s gross. [André] So one of the things that I wasn’t sure about, because later on we learn that, that Anna has just come out of maternity leave. So she’s had a kid, which you see. There’s a photo of the kid a bit further down. Now, I’m unsure, ’cause we weren’t given this level of context, whether she’s been MIA and off the grid, and hasn’t been communicating with her audience, and then this email is maybe the first interaction in a while. I’m not sure about that. But if that is the case, I would change that, “Hey, it’s Anna. I have some exciting news for you.” And then it just leads straight into it. Boom. If there has been a break, I would probably bring that up to the top, that image. And just say, “Hey, it’s Anna. I’ve been on, you know, I’ve been on maternity leave.” And then just catch people up with your life in general. And there’s a photo of the kid. I think everybody would wanna see that. It’s an emotional thing to share. And then just, like, “This is why I’ve been off the grid for the last…” Insert weeks, months, whatever it is. “And I’m using this, now that I can’t wait to get out of my maternity break, the kids, you know, constraints… And we’re now using this as an opportunity to share this masterclass. I can’t wait to get to get into this.” So you have something that’s emotional, that people can bond to. You know, babies, women, and then that frames the reason why this thing is happening. And then, that then becomes the lead just as Shawn mentioned here. So it would push that down a bit, but I think you would hook people in with a picture of a baby. Certainly if you’d been off the scene for a little while. And then, with that attention established, here’s the reason why this email exists. And then, boom. You’ve now got someone’s attention. And they’re gonna start reading forward because presumably the majority of the audience who’s receiving this represent these, you know, women that are looking for the right guy, right? Who’s not an asshole. So I think that’s how I would, I would frame that. You’re looking at the video of this and you can see lots of my comments. That’s just nuances as I was reading it. So we’re not gonna unpick all of those. You can read this document yourself and then just see what I’ve written along the way. Deeper down in bridge email number two… So, so from there. So it starts to show up here, but it’s even further down. She mentions this thing that feels like it’s the big reason, which is this thing called the mirror principle. It’s just a bit further down from what you… There. So the mirror principle. And I went and watched a few videos as well, just to try and wrap my head around what this thing is. And if this is the thing. And the mirror principle seems to be this, this way of seeing the world. This way of showing up in the world. And then, because of the way you show up, it attracts certain men and it pushes the way other people. And then, you know. So that becomes the thing. So that seems like it’s the thing that should be right up on email number one, when you’re first presenting this bridge email. And then, once you have someone’s attention, you connect it to this thing called the mirror principle. Now you’ve got someone’s attention, that element of tension has been created. So this is the tension between A and B. This is now A. You’ve just established this A. You’ve got the right people’s attention, and you’ve now just hooked them with this thing called the mirror principle. And it is what we’re gonna be talking about in this masterclass series. The masterclass series is not, in my mind… It’s not some preregistration… It’s not five preregistration emails and then a webinar. The masterclass series should be the whole thing. It happens to be in five emails, and then there’s a webinar. I think that’s a better way to frame this. So the value starts the moment email number one goes out to this segment of women, at which point the mirror principle starts to get unpacked. But not… See? Shawn’s got it highlighted there. The five steps of the mirror principle, the program. She’s asking people to actually do this homework, do this work. This is at the bottom of the bridge emails, the second bridge email. And that just feels like it’s… To me, it felt too much. It felt like it’s relieving tension because it’s all there. That should be in the series. That’s how I’m seeing this. And the bridge email is really just, is just for somebody… To get them excited about this thing. And that’s it. So I’ll stop rambling now. And then I’ll let you… You’ve had some time to parse some of this stuff out.

[Shawn] Still recovering from your comments earlier. Yeah, I’m gonna… I have a lot of similar thoughts. I’m gonna to see if I can just do a drive-by real fast here. Let’s see. So I guess my first question would be… I think I’m caught in some ways on where we put the picture of why Anna has been out. But then I got hung up lower down, which is the picture. I assume this is Anna and husband, two kids. So I guess the question I have… The first question that I would be curious about, and again, I don’t know the audience, but is this the dream for Anna’s audience? They want the husband and the two kids? Or do they want the guy on, on the beach? Or dressed up, going out to dinner? What’s the imagery that’s the dream? Now, maybe this is the eventual dream, but I don’t know that this is the right-now dream, right? My guess is, again, not knowing this audience at all, but this is future pacing way too far, right? ‘Cause to me, this puts a hell of a lot of weight on the decision. If I’m like, “Wait a minute, if I’ve gotta choose… If I’ve got to figure out in my decision-making the guy who I’m going to, you know, years down the road, have a couple of kids with… Yikes”, right? But maybe that’s the dream. I don’t know. I guess it’s just, to me, it’s like, that feels like it’s farther down the line. So I think this first one, you know, this explains things. Although I’m gonna sound like a total jackass when I say this. Sorry. Take the husband out of the picture, I think. Pick a picture of Anna and the baby. Maybe, right? Again, it’s just, there’s a lot in the photo. And in this photo in particular, the husband is at the center. He’s at the forefront. I don’t think this is about him. I think it’s about Anna, and she’s back, and there’s a baby. And the husband? Sure. He’s in the picture. But I don’t know that he’s the subject of the picture. And he feels like the subject of this picture to me. Again, not the target audience, but just keep that as feedback. These emails are long. Says the guy who cowrites long emails.

[André] They’re definitely too long.

[Shawn] They’re too long in the lead. Like we used to say in journalism, a popular phrase in journalism is, “Don’t bury the lead.” The lead’s buried here, and there are about 12 leads. So I think, I think there are lots of ideas in here. First of all, get rid of this sentence. Nobody knows what a complicated dance polarity is. I get it. I’m sure that it’s inside-the-tent language, but don’t make me think when I’m reading something. I’m like, “Wait a minute. ‘The dance of polarity?’ What the hell are we talking about here?” We gotta keep things a little bit simpler, a little bit punchier. But I love this idea. This is a reframe. And I love reframes, right? “Men compete to get your attention. You’re the one who chooses your mate. It’s been like this for millions of years, and it’s still driving our dating behavior.” But it’s not driving our dating behavior. That’s the thing that’s interesting about it, right? And this is the big secret. And, again, not her audience, but I know enough about evolutionary psychology. And, as long as I can remember, I’ve been a man. And I know, from a male perspective, at least my perspective, men are not the ones who generally feel like they have power in dating relationships. It’s generally… And I say all the time, we’re not the most sophisticated creatures on Earth. We’re not. But, to me, this is a big reframe, right? Because it sounds like women are losing hope. They’re attracting the wrong guys. They wanna know… I think this is the subject. “Where are all the good men?” That question, that’s a subject line.

– That’s a subject line.

– That’s the question. That’s it. That, to me, frames everything. “Where are all the good men?” Because you can do an interesting thing. When you start to frame things a certain way, you can frame it as, “Where are all the good men?” And then you can say… The reframe. Let’s think about this one way. So here’s the conventional framing. “Where are all the good men? Because I, as the woman, need to go out and attract the good men and not deal with all the dumbasses.” Right? But later you say, “Here’s the thing though, as a woman, you choose. Men are competing for your attention. You are actually the one in the position of power. So the question isn’t, where are all the good men? It’s, how do you put yourself out into the world so that the good men show up for you to choose from?” That’s an entirely different perspective. This, to me, feels very conventional, right? It’s like, “Okay, you’ve tried all these things and…” But they’ve tried all of these things because they have the wrong idea. This to me is a perfect example of this. It’s this idea from Rich Schefren, that André knows I just absolutely love. Not to go down too deep into the weeds, but it’s a way to think about marketing where… Rich is the first person I know of who codified this. But the idea is, what you think is the problem is x so you do y, but the real problem is z and y makes z worse. And everyone who’s listening is like, “What the hell did he just say?” What you think is the problem is that you need to go out and be attractive to the good men. And because of that, you do all these things. You look at these tips and tricks, and you read the articles, and you go on all the apps. And you do all these things that are perfectly logical, but the problem is you framed it the wrong way. You’re not out there looking for a good man. You’re making yourself visible and the good men need to show up and find you. So all the things you were doing, because you thought about this the wrong way, are the wrong things and they’re making the situation worse. And once you see reality for what it is, then you realize there are entirely different things that you should be doing. Assuming that this is true, and I believe it’s true… Certainly from my experience on one side of the equation, it is absolutely true that men are competing for the attention of women. That’s what dating feels like, in so far as I can remember what dating ever felt like. But that, in general terms, that seems to be kind of how things work. So this is a perception. This is a framing switch. What they think is the problem is they don’t have the power. So they do all of these things because they don’t think they have the power, but the reality is they have the power. And once they start making decisions from within that framework of having the power, everything changes. That, to me, is what this bridge email is about. And, sure, yeah. Anna had a baby. She’s been gone. That’s great. Not to be a jackass, but, at the end of the day, I’m sure the audience is connected with Anna and that’s important. But this is, this is where I, you know, I think, where I’m a little weird about things. I mean, if this is an apology, get rid of it, right? She’s been out for a while. Or do it differently. I mean, I don’t know where this fits. But, to me, this is like a separate thing. This is almost like another email with no… This isn’t a bridge email. This is a… We don’t have a term for a pre-bridge email. But, to me, this would be… This would almost be an email to set up a bridge email. Like, “Hey, it’s Anna. I’ve been out. Here’s why I’ve been out. I’m so excited. Here’s a picture of me with my baby Eva. And here’s my husband. And this is so great. And now that we’re back, I’ve got so much I wanna share with you. That’s coming up. Psyched to be back.” Good. Step one, Anna is back. Step two, now that Anna’s back, Anna’s got some shit to share with you, right? Boom, step one. Here’s the thing. “Where the hell are all the good men?” Then Anna can… We don’t wanna do 12 things in an email, in a bridge email, because I don’t know what I’m supposed to do with this. Am I supposed to be excited that Anna’s back? Am I supposed to be trying to figure out where the good men are? I don’t know what to do. And we can make it easier for people to know what to do by sequencing this messaging a little bit better, I think. So, step one, Anna’s back. Step two, shit’s about to get real now that Anna’s back. Question number one, “Where the hell are all the good men? Well, that’s what we’re gonna find out.” Boom. And here’s the problem, the reason. So then we tee up, right? “Where are all the good men? That’s the wrong question. You shouldn’t have to be looking for the good men. The good men ought to be looking for you because here’s the deal. Women don’t chase men. Men chase women. That’s the way our species has evolved for millions of years. You can try to fight it all day long. And if you do, you’re not going to get the results that you want. But the moment you realize that for millions of years, men have chased women and they ought to be chasing you, everything changes for the better.” And then you unpack that idea. Because now you have my attention. “Shit. You’re right. I’ve been thinking about this entirely backwards. I’ve been out there looking for the right guys. I’m on the dating apps. I’m reading the articles. You know, I’m looking for the tips and tricks. Nothing works. Well, guess what? It doesn’t work because it’s never going to work because you’re trying to do something that doesn’t make sense. Don’t go chase men. Let them chase you. Let me explain to you how to do that.” That’s it. That’s the bridge. Shit, now I wanna buy this thing. I am certain it’s not the right thing for me.

[André] Yeah, so one idea to, to connect with what you’ve just said is, because this is all date-dependent and this bridge went out on, like, October 28th and 29th… You know, if Anna is back, that could have been in a newsletter that was strategically sent out a few days before this, or some period before this, that’s not too far. You know, where the gap isn’t too far apart. And then it’s the baby pictures. And, you know, wrapped up into a newsletter. If she sends out newsletters. I hope she does. And then, in the P.S. or somewhere further down, it could be, “Hey, watch out for this email that I’m gonna be sending. I’ve got some exciting things to share with you.” Which then kind of gets people curious about this thing. So now, when the bridge comes out, as Shawn’s just said, it can lead straight into this thing and be nice and punchy, and really pull people’s attention and excitement mostly forward. And, you know, you want them to be high-fiving the opt-in button, the raise-hand button. Just clicking on it, double-clicking on it. Hard, you know? So…

[Shawn] Can we measure that somehow? We got a thousand people on our interest list, but 60 of them clicked hard. Sweet.

[André] I think you can on iPhones with their 3D click, right?

– That’s true.

– “This was 3D clicked.”

[Shawn] But this… I feel bad ’cause I’m gonna sound like a jackass, but I’m just gonna say it. You know, no offense to anybody listening to this. Anna, love you. Khara, love you. But, so, you know, we’re starting here talking about something that’s important to your audience, right? That’s how they’re going to be seeing this. And then we make this weird transition, which brings me to the exciting news. “I’ve been out. And I had a baby.” Well, at the risk of sounding like a jackass, that’s exciting news to you, Anna. It’s less exciting news to your audience who was just excited about finally solving this problem that they’re dealing with all day, every day, which they can’t find the guy. Or the guys can’t find them, et cetera. So this, to me… Again, just put this in a different email. Make this in a different email that’s the exciting news about baby Eva. That is exciting in its own context. It’s not exciting, or it’s less exciting, when it’s appended to something that’s about them. Unless I just don’t understand the market, and this is the transition because this is what they really want. And Anna embodies that for them. Then, okay. And before… I’ve gotta mention this paragraph too. Sorry, but I have to. All right. Here’s the paragraph. “It’s been awesome to step back, knowing that my committed masculine man and our tiny-but-mighty team are taking good care of our beautiful claimed community of conscious, wild women.” What the hell is a claimed community? I don’t even know. I don’t even… Maybe it’s just a typo, but I don’t know what that means. To me, this just seems… I put it in a different…

[André] I think Claimed is the brand, maybe.

[Shawn] Oh, is that what it is? That makes sense. All right. All right, see? This is why it’s important, because I can just think out loud and when I’m wrong, I can just say, “Hey, that was dumb.” I don’t know… I would still get rid of it. I mean, if they’re in the community, they don’t need you… They don’t need the name. And I’m not just saying that to defend the position at all. It just doesn’t read well, to me. It just, it’s kinda… Maybe it’s because it’s just out of context. If it were its own email, I don’t know, I just, this… The challenge I have with this is I feel like it… Again, and I’m hypersensitive to this. I don’t… When I’m creating value for somebody, when I’m busting my ass to put something together and bring it out into the world and doing the work, I don’t apologize for that. If I’ve been out, you know, doing something for three months and I just haven’t been paying as much attention as I want to because I just had a child or something, to me, an apology diminishes things. I just, I don’t… Now, I’m not suggesting that, you know, we never apologize. If we’ve done something wrong then we should apologize. Having a baby’s not doing anything wrong, right? You were out on maternity. Awesome. Tell me about that. Earlier, right? Do that earlier and talk about that. And I don’t know that… I mean, maybe if it… Again, if this is important, if your audience wants to be able to have it all, meaning that they want to be able to have the business, and they want to have the man, and they want to have a team that’s making them money even when they’re out, for a great group of conscious, wild women, awesome. Talk about that. But from what I’m understanding, the value that Anna creates, it’s about getting the committed man. It’s not about this other stuff. So I don’t know that the other stuff is as relevant, right? It may be relevant to… This all feels relevant here to Anna. It doesn’t feel relevant to Anna’s audience, beyond this idea, like, “Hey, I’ve been out because, hey, look, it’s baby Eva.” Show a picture, baby Eva with Anna. Maybe just the baby, right? That’s awesome. If your audience is looking for… If primarily what Anna offers is the… is finding the man, not the other business stuff, then talk about how awesome it has been to be able to rely on her committed, masculine man and dimensionalize that. What has he done that’s made her life easier? Why is that so fulfilling, to have this person in her life? When she has been able to do… She’s focusing on having a baby and there’s a bunch of other stuff she can’t focus on. How is he helping with that? Don’t try to talk about the other stuff because the other stuff, unless it’s relevant to something that you offer as a course, or as training, a part of your larger community… If you’re working with women to attract a man, and build a business, and have the communities and all this other stuff, then, sure, it’s relevant. But if you’re not, make it about what’s relevant to them, even though it’s about you. Or her, in this case. I feel like I’m being a jerk about this one. This is, again, this is… It’s not right or wrong. Just one person thinking out loud, that’s all it is. So let’s move on to the second email. You had some fun with the second one.

[André] Yeah, the second one is… It’s just long. And it feels like it’s not true to what a bridge email really is. I mean, I know it’s asking to raise a hand, but it’s unpacking a whole part of this thing, you know? So it’s repeating this thing called the mirror principle, which I think… That should have been revealed right at the very top of the first email, but then not… There’s no need to unpack what that is. Now, the second bridge email unpacks what it is in… I mean, it’s long. There’s two components to it. There’s images and then there’s homework. And then the homework unpacks it even further. And don’t forget, this bridge email is now going to everybody. It feels too much. It feels like everybody is being exposed to this email. And if it’s not relevant to them, it’s kind of… It’s in their face, kind of. It should be less than that, I feel. Obviously, connected to the first bridge. So it’s continuing and it’s also, it’s doing the job of what a bridge does. It’s putting people’s attention forward if they’re interested. So I think just hinting at this thing called the mirror principle, of which this whole masterclass series is about… So if you’re interested in getting… You know, “Where are all the good men?” If you’re interested in solving that problem, there’s this thing called the mirror principle that I’m gonna share with you that does all these… That’s gonna help you show up in such a way that the guy’s gonna be, you know, just… You’re gonna get more of the right guys showing up, so then you get to pick them. As I understand it. So all that stuff you’re scrolling past there, just seems like a lot for a bridge email.

[Shawn] Listening and working at the same time. Yeah, I’m stuck on the P.S. here. Sorry, this is the first thing. I said we were gonna move on to the second one and I lied. So, sorry. What in the world? All right. Let’s get to the P.S. All right. So here’s the P.S. “A disclaimer. Definitely don’t raise your hand for this, unless you’re ready for a major paradigm shift from soul-destroying and stressful dates to attracting a totally different caliber of men, as Anna S. experienced. She went from…” Okay. This feels to me like the job interview question, right? Of course, I haven’t had a job in 25 years, but, you know. It’s like the, you know, “Tell me something about yourself.” You know, “Tell me a…” I can’t even remember the question. Basically, “Tell me a problem with you.” And you’re like, “You know what? I just work too damn hard. I’m just too good at what I do.” This P.S. feels that way to me. It’s like, “Hey, a disclaimer. Don’t click on this. Don’t raise your hand for this, unless you’re ready for something that’s so badass, your world will be turned upside down.” That feels like selling. Don’t do that. Don’t do that, right? And… Sure, you can say, “P.S. a disclaimer.” All right, but… You can say, “Don’t raise your hand for this…” Or, “Don’t raise your hand for this, unless…” And then, just don’t… It feels sales-y to me, right? It just… Because who doesn’t want this? What you’re saying is, “Okay, if you don’t raise your hand for this, what you’re really saying is that you’re cool with soul-destroying, stressful dates.” Like, no. That’s not what anybody’s saying when they don’t click. They’re saying that right now’s not a good time for them. Or this isn’t the most important thing to them right now. So I think you can get more people to click on this because nobody wants to say no to this, right? No one wants to say, “No, no, no, it’s cool. I’m still comfortable with soul-destroying, stressful dates for now. I’m fine with that.” No. That’s not what people are saying. So when you frame it that way, you frame it… This, to me, feels like the used-car-salesman disclaimer, where you walk on the lot and the used-car salesman walks up and is, like, “Hey, don’t take another step unless you’re ready to get the best deal of your life.” And you’re like, “Just, please, God, kill me”, right? And you hate it. It feels gross. This disclaimer feels gross to me. Don’t do that. Do it in a way that… You can do the same thing. “Don’t raise your hand for this if now’s not a great time for you. And here’s how to know…” Or, you know, “P.S. a disclaimer, this isn’t going to be right for everybody right now. And that’s perfectly okay.” Let them know it’s fine if they say no. “Here’s how to know if this is right for you right now.” And then just tell them, right? And then use Anna as a reference. Right? Or not. I would probably lose this. But if you keep this thing with Anna here, what I would do is I would probably get rid of the red box and I would get rid of the last paragraph. The last paragraph is a real tension reducer. Because here’s what I wanted. I’ll just read this for everybody quickly. This is Anna, posting in August. “I have just got back into dating after some time away, and since starting this program.” Nice framing, right? She’s been away from dating. She’s back. She’s started the program, so it’s going to be different. “I posted something several days ago moaning about online dating and how much I disliked it. I ended up deleting it because I was having a bad day last week and feeling overly negative about a lot of things.” Okay. Anna’s a human being, just like the rest of us. I can identify with Anna. I’ve done that too. I’ve bitched and moaned about things and I’m like, “Why am I doing this?” And deleting it. So now Anna and I, we sort of understand each other, right? Her experience and my experience, we’re both human. Got it. Next. “Anyway”, nice transition, “today I went on my first online date since doing this program and, wow. This guy was so masculine, I felt like I could relax. He planned the place, got there quite early, saw it was actually closed, found another place, texted me in time before I even left home, paid for my drink, ordered what I wanted, walked me to my car, asked thoughtful questions, and showed interest in me.” Done. Because now I want to know what’s going on. Like, did that turn into something? ‘Cause I’m assuming her audience wants to find that guy. Hell, I wanna find that guy. I’d like somebody to pay for my drink and order what I want, walk me to my car and ask thoughtful questions. But the transition sucks. Because, “This has never happened for me before.” No, I’m sorry, keep the next part. “This has never happened before, that a guy on a date does all of that for me. In the past, I’d be the one choosing the place, arranging things. The guy often shows up late and gives off a wishy-washy vibe.” Stop right there because the next sentence sucks. “I’m not sure if I was attracted to him physically.” Dun-dun-duuun. Like, oh. She got, she got all these great things, but now we all have an image in our mind of, like, “Oh, it’s that guy. He did everything right, but he’s not particularly attractive. Shit.” Right? So… And my guess is, lots of people now are having that visceral reaction, like, “Ugh. Great. So you got a guy that was almost everything, but not something that actually matters.” Like, just don’t… And maybe it feels disingenuous and you should keep it. But if it feels disingenuous to remove the end, don’t include the whole thing because the end, to me, makes that whole thing irrelevant. What essentially Anna is saying, if I were able to paraphrase it, is, “I haven’t been dating. I just got back after starting this program. I was in a bad place, but I went on this date and, damn, it was incredible. Except I’m not sure the dude’s very good looking.” That’s really what this says. So it’s like, “Huh. Everything was okay, but I don’t know if I’m really attracted to the guy yet.” It’s not a great testimonial. Whereas, if you reverse the testimonial, and let’s just say a thousand people read this, how many… Of those thousand, how many people is their dream that they could find the guy who’s masculine, they could relax around him, he is taking charge, he ordered their drink, he’s thoughtful, he shows interest. But he’s not good looking. He’s not attractive. Nobody has that dream. They want all of the first part, and they want the guy to be good-looking. And if they don’t have all of it, then it’s not a win. That’s the reality. It sounds like I’m being overly simplistic, but that’s just… From the audience perspective, I don’t think this testimonial’s gonna light somebody up. Like, “Oh, I can’t wait to find a guy who’s masculine and takes charge and I’m not really attracted to.” Anyway.

[André] Then… Scroll back up again. I like, I like the idea. I mean, I’ve done P.S.s similar in the sense that it cues up something that I wanna show. And that can work really well. And this isn’t the same Anna. That’s a customer, or a prospect. Anna S. The main Anna is Anna Rova. So it’s not the same person. So… But because, you know, good-looking, not-good looking, is a subjective quality, I think it would be okay to, you know, maybe take away the red like Shawn suggested. And then that last paragraph, if you didn’t want to cut it out, just blur it out. I blur out many of the screenshots, either for privacy reasons or because it’s just not relevant to the piece. Or, you know, in instances like this, it takes something away that is subjective. One person’s ugliness is another person’s beautiful. But, still, it doesn’t need to be there. So you can just maybe blur out the last piece and then make some amendments to the disclaimer to connect the two nicely.

[Shawn] All right. Let’s move on to the last one here. So you got wound up on the mirror principle. This seems like work. Damn

– Yeah, this is work.

[Shawn] Look at this. Holy shit. I mean, damn. Damn. That’s a lot of work. I’m not doing that work. Even if I want to, what am I gonna do? Should I schedule an afternoon to do this? Wow. You’re absolutely right. This should all be… I mean, talk about that you’re going to cover this in the registration leading up to the… This is all stuff in the series that leads up to the webinar. But, whoa, what an email. This is a lot. This is three, four emails.

[André] Yeah.

[Shawn] I don’t… Can we get up this… Sorry. You start talking. I’m gonna react to this.

[André] So I think, you know, bridge email one and two gets people’s attention and excitement about the mirror principle idea. That’s the only reason those bridges are there. And then, they’re coming into… The framing should be clearer, in the sense that… At least, this is my point of view here, is that the entire thing is a series. So it’s a masterclass series that happens to be… The first part is email and the second part is a webinar. But both together make up a series. They’re not separate things. And then, maybe… I mean, I don’t know enough about these parts, but I noticed here that she’s mentioned that mirror principle and then there’s these five components of it that’s been listed in this bridge email further down. Maybe one of those per email in the main series just unpacks these things. And instead of getting somebody to take out her pen and paper and do this entire exercise, each email in the series… So email one, which is, “Observe”, would just unpack that. And then, “Now go do the work.” Some of the work that’s going to reveal something about that principle, that, “Observe”. Whatever that is. And then there’ll be some sort of insight. Then the people are interested to know what’s the second lesson or the second principle. And it’s, “Identify”. And then the second email in the series. So by the time the webinar shows up after the five emails, people have a frame of reference now. They know what these five things are and they’re excited about it. And then the webinar just ties up all the loose ends and it’s an opportunity to connect, to see people. And then do what the webinar does. I think that’s[Shawn] Getting sucked in here. Yeah.

[André] A lot of pictures that are attracting you.

[Shawn] Yeah. It’s funny. It’s why I don’t wanna read them in advance, because it’s hard to have that first reaction. Because if you think about it, most people aren’t studying. Well, that’s not true. We get people who seem to study our emails, but people tend to, like… Their first impressions are quick. Subconscious. We make sense of the world around us with things like, “How long is this gonna take?” and, “Wow, there’s a lot here.” To reflect that, it’s important. But then I’m just, you know, I’m looking at, I’m looking at some of the info here and thinking about something we said earlier too. I would make one of these things, one of these sentences… So Anna says, through Khara and her writing, “I used to live in a world where…” And then she has these quotes. “You can never trust men. They will always let you down. Men are afraid of commitment. They only want one thing. It’s nice guy versus assholes and there’s nothing in between. Men cheat, lie, are lazy, selfish, and so on. What changed? The answer is me. My belief system about men.” That’s a reframe. It’s a really powerful reframe. It should be the logical conclusion, or the logical extension, of the reframe from the previous email. It doesn’t need to be repeated. We should be able to just hang it on the previous email, because you’ve already… The first email frames everything that follows. You can refer back to the frame, but you’ve established the frame. And that frame… Again, I think the frame here, really, is that women have the power. This seems to continue this thing because women have power over their belief system about men. We’re pointing in that direction. So I would… You wanna get attention with the subject line. I would use something… I think that subject line I would choose would be something like, “Men cheat, lie…” Or, you know, “Men are late”. Something that’s basically, “Men cheat, lie, are lazy, selfish.” I would have that as the subject line, dot, dot, dot, and then start with, “I used to believe that. I used to believe that too.” Okay. “What the hell is going on here? Now you have my attention.” Because that subject line then connects to the thing. But then you make that switch, and the switch lives within the previous frame. “I used to believe that about men too, but the reality is… I used to believe that about men too. Along with…” And then just make a list. “You can never trust men. They’ll always let you down. Men are afraid of commitment. They only want one thing. It’s nice guys versus assholes.” Then make the list. If you’ve made that, make the transition that way, Grab my attention with the subject line, keep it going in the initial body. Imply. When you say, “I used to believe that too”, that’s an open loop. It’s an implication, right? ‘Cause if you used to believe it, you no longer believe it. Well, why don’t you believe it now? Then you don’t tell ’em yet because you also used to believe a lot of other things. Right? “One, two, three, four. Here’s what they are.” But, again, it’s framed with the implication that you used to believe this. And you used to believe all of these other things, which means that you no longer believe them. And if you don’t believe them now, why? What the hell changed? Well, what changed was her belief system. And that’s… You can see how it’s pulling attention down very simply using some of these principles of serialized narratives, where you are… It’s such a powerful hook, but there’s way, way, way, way too much here. So I think the story in this email is… The attention getter is, basically, “Men are these five bad things.” Whatever. “They cheat, they lie, they’re lazy, they’re selfish. They smell bad.” Whatever. Men are all these things. Dot, dot, dot. Then dot, dot, dot again. Ellipsis. “I used to believe that too.” Implication, “Holy shit. Now you’ve got my attention.” “I also used to believe…” And then you’ve got the list. And most likely the women who are reading this are nodding along too, saying, “Yeah. “I believe this too.” Then you need to make the switch. But it’s implied she doesn’t believe that now. What changed? Well, what changed was that the mirror principle got her to see things differently. So then you describe the mirror principle at a 10,000-foot view, right? “This is the mirror principle. This is what I did. It shows, you know, whatever. It led me to facing two difficult truths.” “Ah, okay. Interesting. Difficult.” It’s not a formal open loop. I don’t know if it’s an implication. It’s an implication. Two difficult truths mean, like, “Ooh, these are probably not obvious. There’s probably going to be some explanation required.” Difficult implies some things, right? Get rid of the images here. They just break things up. I don’t know if that’s Anna. The images are confusing. Tell me what the difficult truths are, but don’t over explain what the difficult truths are. Get my attention. Hint that we’re gonna talk about these in more detail later. But tell me what they are. And make me feel them. And make me understand why those truths contribute to me feeling bad. If I believe these things… Or, no, sorry. These are two difficult truths that she had to face. So by facing these truths, how do I feel differently? What changed? ‘Cause if we talk about you have to make a… This is where it gets confusing. We have two difficult truths and then we have a first fundamental shift. I assume that I have to make a fundamental shift in response to a difficult truth. Most people aren’t going to do that heavy lifting for you. You’re gonna have to make that connection for ’em. “Face these two difficult truths and here’s what they are.” Boom, boom. “And once you understand these truths, you then have to make two fundamental shifts in response.” Okay. Shift number one, I assume it’s everything in your life … At this point, I don’t know where the actual… How things fit. But that’s the structure of this piece. You’re pulling people down. If you think about it, this, kind of, it flows, right? It’s like, okay, here’s something that your audience will nod to. Like, “Yeah, you know, if we’re being honest with each other, just hanging out, I think men are, you know…” Whatever, “They lie, cheat steal, lazy, selfish, smell bad.” Like, “Yeah, I do too.” And then Anna says, “I used to believe that too. I also used to believe all these other things.” What she’s saying parenthetically is that you also probably believe here now. “But once I realized these two…” Realized, discovered, whatever it is. “These two fundamental truths. Once I faced these two difficult truths, everything changed for me. Here’s what they were. One,” explain it. “Two,” explain it. “In response…” I wouldn’t write it this way, but, “After recognizing these difficult truths, I had a choice to make. I could continue to, I could continue to live the way that I was living before and get the same results that I got before, which is not what I wanted. Or I could make the decision to make… I could make two fundamental shifts that transform everything.” Excuse me. “Here’s shift number one.” Two paragraphs, not 50. “Here’s shift number two.” Two paragraphs, not 50. Then you mention, “In the upcoming masterclass, I’m going to go into detail about, you know, not only why this is important, but how you can make these shifts yourself so that everything on the other side of those two decisions is better.” That’s what this thing is. The rest of it… Because that creates tension, right? Now I wanna go. ‘Cause now, in order to relieve the tension, I need to go attend the masterclass ’cause I want these things really bad. I want to understand these truths and I want to know how to make these fundamental shifts. I want that because I want the thing on the other side of it. And Anna’s convinced me, and Khara’s convinced me, that in order to get the thing on the other side of it, I have to change doing what I was doing before. What I was doing before is not going to get me there because of these reasons. I have to make these changes, but I don’t know how. In order to find out how, I need to go… I’m gonna go listen to this webinar. I’m going to go attend this masterclass. And then the intensification series for the masterclass begins to go into the detail around these things without giving it away, without relieving the tension. But by setting up what’s going to be discussed in the masterclass, and framing it in a certain way to the benefit of the audience, then all of the tension is focused on this event of the masterclass, where I’m finally going to understand how to make these shifts. These fundamental shifts. But the way this is written, I could look at this and be like, “Whoa. I agree 100%. It looks like everything I need to do is all here. Like, I got this… I got the… I can fix this, right? ‘Five steps to fixing your dating patterns with men.’ Sweet, right? It’s all here. Why do I need to go to the masterclass? She’s gonna try to sell me something in the masterclass anyway. I’ll just do this instead.” That’s what your audience is thinking when they see this. Take all of this out. All of it. Goodbye. Okay. That felt like a nice rant. What else you got?

[André] Yeah, that’s good. Yeah, I mean, everything that you’ve said. There’s, you know, there’s a version that in some contexts can work really well, is to use… You can use two archetypes to paint this picture early on. So, you know, it could either be, you know, something that’s happened to Anna herself before she got her husband. So her reflecting back. Or she could be reflecting through a story from one of her clients. And, you know, so, “When I met a version of Greg.” And then that story starts to play out. You can write it like a narrative. There could be some dialogue. This is just representing an archetype of these people that fall into this bucket called “Greg”, for example. So people can then, who are reading it, can relate to, “Oh, yeah. I’ve just picked up a Greg last week and he was an asshole.” So people can start to relate to narratives in a way that’s not quite the same when it’s just your voice writing an email. And then, when that transition happens where she talks about, “There was this shift”, then it’s like, “Well, when I met James…” Or whatever, you know. Maybe this was when she met her husband. I don’t know. Or, again, if this has been reflected through somebody else, it’s just… It’s a group of people that represent this thing, this person. But it’s only different because of the shifts she made. And she’s still the same person, but because of her beliefs, changed through this mirror-principle thing, she attracted this other group of people that are represented with this other character. So, you know, it’s unnecessary if it’s too difficult to write, but I think if you, if you can write one of those, they can work ridiculously well. Yeah. So I think that’s the only thing I’ll add, but it’s not required to do what she wants. Just unpack.

[Shawn] I think I would come at the characters probably… This is a both-and thing. So you can certainly do it that way. I think I would come at it where the characters were the women. And the characters were the two… In many ways, Anna fills this, this role for our audience. But I would contrast the two types of women. You can think of it as, like, pre-frame, pre paradigm shift, post paradigm shift, right? So there’s, whatever, Jane who thinks all men are useless. On and on. All the stuff that was said at the beginning of this email. But she thinks that way… And then we can sort of draw… That archetype thinks that way for all of these reasons. But then there’s whoever. The Anna version who understands that it’s… That she’s the creative force in her life. She’s the one who attracts men or doesn’t. The reason I liked having the archetype be women is that it makes the power more accessible to the audience. So it’s not just who they attract. And I don’t know the audience, so maybe that’s better. But it’s who they become. My sense is that’s what the program is about, is the changes that they need to make to attract different people in their lives. So I would focus on who they become. I think that’s how I would approach it. And I’ve heard this before, and this sounds wildly ridiculous from a man saying this, so, please, everyone who’s hearing this, realize I mean this with positive intent, but I’ve heard somewhere some reference to that women tend to look at other women more than women tend to look at men. Just in a purely referential standpoint. Like, in a social setting, how are the other women dressed versus how are the men dressed? Which makes sense. It makes perfect sense. So I think having women characters that another woman, the woman leader… This would be true for men too, I think. But for a woman reading it to see the person who she wants to be in a character versus who she doesn’t want to be, I think that might be more powerful than having who she would attract. I don’t know, that’s two different ways to think about it.

[André] I agree, I agree. I’m gonna change my mind now. So I’m gonna go-

– You can do that?

– The women archetypes. Yeah, you do it all the time, so I’m going to do the same.

[Shawn] That’s true. Strong convictions, loosely held. Yeah. Yeah. Here’s the thing. I’m not gonna read through this entire testimonial here. Just be cautious. I guess this is what I’ll wrap up with, ’cause I know we’ve been going for a while, but you need to know what the thing is about either before you write it or after you’ve written it. To me, this is a great draft. There’s tons of material in here and there’s no… We can’t overstate the value of actually doing the work. So don’t take any of this as critical. This is just, how can we make it better? But you need a thing first to be made better. So just take all the commentary and feedback within that lens. You’ve done the work, this is just commentary about work done. But I would figure out what this is about. What’s the controlling idea? I would make this three emails, not two. I don’t know if this has already been sent, but I would make this… If I were doing this, it would be three emails, not two. Email number one is, “Anna’s back and here’s why this is great. Woo-hoo, this is awesome. And now that Anna’s back, shit’s about to get real.” That’s the first one, right? Things are about to happen. Anna’s had some time off. She’s had plenty of time to be thinking about things. She’s had lots of time to realize how great life is for her because of some decisions that she’s made. Excellent. “Anna’s back.” That’s email one. Email two, “Now that Anna’s back, we’re gonna start talking about some stuff, and here’s…” And then she’s gonna frame it. And what she’s realized since she’s been out, is that there’s a paradigm problem, right? Women think that they’re… They don’t realize that they have all the power in the dating relationship. And when you frame it that way, everything that follows makes sense. Once women realize that they have the power in the dating space, that they can… When they have the power, then they can make changes to themselves. That’s what’s going to change their reality. That’s the frame. That’s the controlling idea. Everything needs to fit within that frame. It needs to lead from one to the next to the next, so it’s a congruent, cohesive, well-structured argument. Even though you’re not framing it as an argument, you’re still making a case. And that case is, “You’re thinking about this the wrong way. Because you’re thinking about this the wrong way, you’re having all these problems. The moment you think about it the right way, all of those problems go away and all the solutions become available to you. And we’re going to talk about what some of those are and we’re gonna get you so you understand them. But then to really do it right, we’re gonna spend 90 minutes together in a masterclass and we’re going to go into detail about this. That’s the series. That’s what these bridge emails are.

– Yeah, so-

– André, take us home.

[André] So there’s… According to this Google Doc, there’s 59 pages of stuff here. And we’ve spent all of our time on the first two emails, the bridge emails, so… Oh, wow, look at that. You know, having a very successful promotion starts with the framing of a really good bridge bridge series, whether it’s one bridge email or two bridge emails. Or it’s been set up in a previous email, whether it’s a newsletter or something. But we’ve spent all this time. I don’t know how long we’ve been rambling on now for, just for these bridge emails. You can see that, that the entire structure is different from our point of view now. And I think the next time they do this promotion, if they model some of what we’ve been saying here, it’ll be interesting to contrast their results to, you know, what happened with this version. I think that’ll be an interesting insight. But I think, broadly speaking, to make this valuable to everybody that’s, you know, that’s not necessarily in the dating space, is just to really figure out the strategy behind what you’re saying, the controlling idea, the theme, how this is going to be structured, and then do it in a way, using these bridge emails to really pull people’s attention and get them excited. Establish that point of tension that only the series or the thing behind this, you know, whatever it is, is gonna resolve, you know. This is the start of establishing that tension. And as soon as we relieve tension inside of the thing that we’re trying to get attention from, then that’s a red herring. That’s a signal that there’s some work that needs to be done. So, yeah, I think that’s, that’s my last words. Any comments?

– Yeah. This was a fun one. Thanks, Khara, for the opportunity to do this. This is… It’s a big deal. I mean, I know I spent hours yesterday trying to craft a base of a bridge email unsuccessfully. This is hard. It’s hard work. And I’ll be 100% upfront. I don’t want anybody to see that work, right? I would not want to put that out in the world and get feedback on it. So please hear our feedback for what it is. It’s feedback. We don’t know your audience. We don’t know the context. You do. A lot of what we said may not be accurate, may not fit just right. But mine it for what’s useful and see what happens. But you know better about this than we do. And recognize, everybody listening to this, this is hard work. On that note, thanks everybody.

[André] Thanks, everyone.
